content.text: What Catastrophic Forgetting Is

Catastrophic forgetting is the tendency of a neural network to abruptly and severely lose performance on a previously learned task when it is trained on a new one. Unlike human memory, which integrates new information while retaining old knowledge, gradient descent in a shared parameter space causes new task updates to overwrite the weight configurations that encoded the prior task.
The phenomenon was documented by McCloskey and Cohen (1989) in connectionist models and remains a fundamental challenge in
continual learning

— the setting where a model must learn from a non-stationary stream of tasks without revisiting earlier data. In the fine-tuning context, even a single additional fine-tuning run can degrade a model's general benchmark performance by 5–30%, depending on how different the new task is and how aggressively it is trained.

attrs.alt: Performance curves showing Task A accuracy collapsing during Task B fine-tuning, with EWC mitigation, and a comparison grid of five mitigation strategies

content.text: Why It Happens

All tasks share the same weight space. When gradient descent minimises the loss for task B, it moves parameters toward a local minimum for B. That minimum is typically far from the minimum for task A — the two loss surfaces do not naturally align. The magnitude of forgetting is determined by the
overlap between the gradient directions
of the two tasks: if task B requires pushing the same weights in opposite directions from where task A needed them, A's accuracy collapses.
Three factors amplify the severity:
Task dissimilarity

: adding a code generation task to a sentiment classifier causes more forgetting than adding a related classification head. Distant tasks compete for different representations and overwrite each other.
Learning rate and epochs
: a high LR or many epochs moves the parameters further from the prior minimum. Even a small dataset fine-tuned for 10 epochs at LR=1e-4 causes catastrophic forgetting in a 7B model.
Full parameter update
: updating all model weights gives the new task complete freedom to repurpose every parameter. PEFT methods structurally prevent this by freezing most weights.
Measuring Forgetting
The standard continual learning metrics quantify forgetting precisely:
Metric
Formula
What it measures
Backward Transfer (BWT)
BWT = (1/T) Σ (Aᵢ,T - Aᵢ,ᵢ)
Average drop in task i accuracy after learning all subsequent tasks. Negative = forgetting.
Forward Transfer (FWT)
FWT = (1/T) Σ (Aᵢ,ᵢ₋₁ - A⁰ᵢ)
How much prior learning helps future tasks (positive = beneficial transfer).
Intransigence
A⁰ᵢ - Aᵢ,ᵢ
Inability to learn new task due to prior training (overregularisation side-effect).

In practice, for production fine-tuning (not full continual learning), track at minimum: (1) performance on the new task's validation set, and (2) scores on 2–3 general benchmarks (e.g. HellaSwag, ARC-Easy) before and after fine-tuning. A drop > 5% on general benchmarks is the standard threshold that triggers investigation.
Mitigation 1: Data Replay
The simplest and most effective mitigation is
experience replay

: include a proportion of prior task data in the new fine-tuning batch. Each gradient step sees both old and new tasks simultaneously, preventing the weight space from shifting entirely toward the new minimum.

from torch.utils.data import ConcatDataset, DataLoader, WeightedRandomSampler

def replay_dataloader(
    new_task_data,
    old_task_data,
    new_fraction: float = 0.8,   # 80% new task, 20% old
    batch_size: int = 16,
) -> DataLoader:
    """Mix new and old task data with a fixed ratio."""
    n_new = len(new_task_data)
    n_old = len(old_task_data)

    # Weight so that expected proportion matches new_fraction
    old_fraction = 1.0 - new_fraction
    w_new = new_fraction / n_new
    w_old = old_fraction / n_old

    weights = [w_new] * n_new + [w_old] * n_old
    combined = ConcatDataset([new_task_data, old_task_data])
    sampler  = WeightedRandomSampler(weights, num_samples=len(combined), replacement=True)

    return DataLoader(combined, batch_size=batch_size, sampler=sampler)

# Example: fine-tune on medical QA while replaying 20% general instruction data
loader = replay_dataloader(
    new_task_data=medical_qa_train,
    old_task_data=general_instruction_data,
    new_fraction=0.80,
)

The replay fraction is a direct control knob: 10% replay preserves general capability with minimal impact on new task convergence speed; 30–50% replay more aggressively defends against forgetting but slows new task learning.
Mitigation 2: Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC)
EWC (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017) adds a regularisation penalty that discourages large changes to the parameters that were most important for the prior task. Importance is measured by the
Fisher information matrix
— the diagonal of the Hessian of the prior task's loss — which quantifies how sensitive the loss was to each parameter:
where
F_i
is the Fisher information for parameter i,
θ*
is the parameter value after training task A, and λ controls the strength of the forgetting penalty.
EWC's main limitation is that the diagonal Fisher approximation is computed over the entire prior task dataset — expensive for large LLMs. In practice,
online EWC
accumulates a running Fisher estimate across mini-batches to reduce this cost.
Mitigation 3: Learning without Forgetting (LwF)
LwF (Li & Hoiem, 2016) avoids the need to store old data by using the
old model as a teacher

. Before fine-tuning on task B, save a copy of the model as a frozen teacher. During task B training, add a distillation loss that encourages the new model's outputs on new-task inputs to match the frozen teacher's outputs. This forces the model to preserve prior behaviour even on data the prior task never saw explicitly:

import copy
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

# 1. Snapshot the model before new task training
teacher = copy.deepcopy(model).eval()
for p in teacher.parameters():
    p.requires_grad_(False)

def lwf_loss(
    student_logits: torch.Tensor,
    teacher_logits: torch.Tensor,
    temperature: float = 2.0,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """KL divergence between student and teacher predictions (soft targets)."""
    p_teacher = F.softmax(teacher_logits / temperature, dim=-1)
    log_p_student = F.log_softmax(student_logits / temperature, dim=-1)
    return F.kl_div(log_p_student, p_teacher, reduction="batchmean") * (temperature ** 2)

# In training loop:
def training_step(model, teacher, batch, lam_lwf=0.5):
    input_ids = batch["input_ids"].to("cuda")
    labels    = batch["labels"].to("cuda")

    # New task loss
    outputs      = model(input_ids=input_ids, labels=labels)
    task_b_loss  = outputs.loss

    # LwF distillation loss (no labels needed)
    with torch.no_grad():
        teacher_out = teacher(input_ids=input_ids)
    distill_loss = lwf_loss(outputs.logits, teacher_out.logits)

    return task_b_loss + lam_lwf * distill_loss
Mitigation 4: LoRA and Structural Isolation

The most practical mitigation for production LLM fine-tuning is LoRA (and PEFT methods generally). When base model weights are frozen and only low-rank adapter matrices are trained, catastrophic forgetting is structurally impossible: the base weights that encoded prior knowledge are never updated.
For multi-task serving, each task gets its own set of LoRA adapters. At inference time, the base model is loaded once and the task-specific adapter is swapped in — a pattern sometimes called
adapter hotswapping

from peft import PeftModel
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

# Load base once
base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")

# Each task's LoRA is trained independently
# During inference: swap the adapter without touching base weights
def predict(task: str, prompt: str) -> str:
    adapter_path = f"adapters/{task}"
    model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, adapter_path, is_trainable=False)
    inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=100)
    return tokenizer.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True)

# All tasks share base weights; no task can overwrite another's knowledge
sentiment_answer = predict("sentiment", "Review: Great product!")
ner_answer        = predict("ner",       "John lives in Paris.")

The trade-off is capacity: a LoRA adapter with r=8 adds ~4M parameters for a 7B model (0.06%). If the task requires deep representational changes that the frozen base cannot express through low-rank modulation, accuracy will lag behind full fine-tuning. In practice this matters most for domain shifts so extreme that the base model's representations are fundamentally misaligned (e.g. training an English model to write code in a language it has never seen).
When Forgetting Matters vs When It Does Not
Scenario
Forgetting risk
Why / recommendation
Single task FT on large, related dataset
Low
Task is close to pre-training; forgetting minimal with low LR
Instruction FT on diverse data
Low–Medium
Diversity of instruction data preserves general capability; LoRA preferred
Narrow task FT (1 task, small dataset)
High
Use LoRA or add 10–20% general replay data
Sequential FT on multiple distinct tasks
Very high
Use per-task LoRA adapters or EWC between each task
CPT on highly specialised domain
Medium–High
Mix 10–30% general corpus; monitor benchmarks at each checkpoint
RLHF / reward modelling after SFT
Medium
KL penalty in PPO objective explicitly constrains drift from SFT policy
Diagnosing Forgetting in Practice
A minimal forgetting diagnostic suite runs three evaluations before and after each fine-tuning run:

from lm_eval import evaluator

def forgetting_diagnostic(model_before_path: str, model_after_path: str) -> dict:
    """
    Compare general benchmark scores before and after fine-tuning.
    Returns forgetting percentages per task.
    """
    tasks = ["hellaswag", "arc_easy", "winogrande"]

    results_before = evaluator.simple_evaluate(
        model="hf",
        model_args=f"pretrained={model_before_path}",
        tasks=tasks,
        batch_size=16,
    )["results"]

    results_after = evaluator.simple_evaluate(
        model="hf",
        model_args=f"pretrained={model_after_path}",
        tasks=tasks,
        batch_size=16,
    )["results"]

    forgetting = {}
    for task in tasks:
        before = results_before[task]["acc,none"]
        after  = results_after[task]["acc,none"]
        delta  = (after - before) / before * 100
        forgetting[task] = round(delta, 2)
        print(f"{task}: {before:.3f} → {after:.3f} ({delta:+.1f}%)")

    return forgetting

# Example output:
# hellaswag: 0.794 → 0.751 (-5.4%)   ← concerning
# arc_easy:  0.821 → 0.819 (-0.2%)   ← acceptable
# winogrande: 0.712 → 0.701 (-1.5%)  ← acceptable

Run this diagnostic before deploying any fine-tuned model to production. A drop of > 5% on any task is an orange flag; > 10% is a red flag that requires either switching to LoRA, adding replay data, or reducing the number of training epochs.
Rehearsal Buffer Management
When old task data is available but large (millions of examples), storing all of it in a replay buffer is impractical.
Reservoir sampling
maintains a fixed-size buffer B that represents a uniform random sample of all data seen so far — without ever growing the buffer beyond its memory budget:

Reservoir sampling provides a statistical guarantee: after processing N examples total with a buffer of size B, each example has probability B/N of being in the buffer. This means the buffer always represents a uniform sample of prior experience, regardless of the ordering or size of the data stream. A buffer of 10k examples is sufficient for most domain adaptation tasks to provide meaningful forgetting mitigation.
Forgetting vs. Intransigence: The Plasticity–Stability Dilemma
Continual learning mitigations introduce a tension called the
plasticity–stability dilemma
. Stability (not forgetting old tasks) and plasticity (learning new tasks quickly) are in direct conflict:
High λ in EWC
: protects old weights strongly → model cannot move far enough to learn the new task well. Performance on new task suffers (intransigence).
Low λ in EWC
: the model can move freely → learns new task well but forgets old tasks (catastrophic forgetting).
Large replay buffer fraction (50%)
: strong retention but new task convergence is slow and requires more steps.
Small replay buffer fraction (5%)
: fast new task convergence but weaker retention.

For production fine-tuning where the new task is the commercial priority, tune the mitigation strength (λ or replay fraction) to keep general benchmark regression under 5% while maximising new task accuracy. Use your forgetting diagnostic on a held-out evaluation set to calibrate — run a grid search over λ ∈ {100, 500, 1000, 5000} or replay fraction ∈ {0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30}.
Summary

Catastrophic forgetting occurs when gradient updates for a new task overwrite the weight configurations that encoded prior task knowledge. It is measured by Backward Transfer (BWT) — the drop in prior task accuracy after new training.
Severity increases with task dissimilarity, learning rate, number of epochs, and full (vs. LoRA) parameter updates.
Data replay (20–30% old task data in new batches) is the simplest and most effective mitigation when old data is available.

EWC adds a Fisher-weighted L2 penalty on important parameters — effective when old data cannot be stored. The diagonal Fisher approximation is expensive for large models; online EWC amortises this cost.
LwF uses the pre-fine-tuning model as a frozen teacher and adds a KL distillation loss on new-task inputs — no old data required.
LoRA structurally prevents forgetting by freezing base weights. For multi-task serving, train a separate adapter per task and hotswap them at inference time over a shared base model.
Always run a forgetting diagnostic (general benchmark before vs. after) before deploying a fine-tuned model. A > 5% drop is a signal to add mitigation.
